references.name: Glossier
references.takeaway: Build the interface on a two-tone pastel palette (primary pastel + white) with product/content photography always shot on matching solid-color backgrounds for a candy-counter uniformity.

influence.palette: The entire interface is built on a two-tone foundation: warm cream (#f5f0e8) paired with clean white (#ffffff). Every card, panel, and surface exists in one of these two tones, creating the candy-counter uniformity described in the takeaway. The cream background acts like Glossier's blush pink — a warm, enveloping base that makes the white surface cards pop without harsh contrast.
influence.surfaces: All metric cards (specials board), project cards (menu board), and content panels use white on cream backgrounds — the same two-tone approach Glossier uses for product display against pastel backdrops.
influence.copyTone: UI copy is conversational and warm ('Good morning, Baker!', 'Pouring your order...', 'Today's Specials') — mirroring Glossier's friendly, advice-like voice rather than corporate language.

references.name: Sweetgreen
references.takeaway: Display items as individual ingredients on a clean white counter — each component isolated with its own label, arranged in a build-your-own composition that invites mixing and matching.

influence.schemaPlanner: The schema planner view directly applies Sweetgreen's ingredient-forward display. Each entity (Recipe, Ingredient, Supplier, Baker) is presented as its own isolated 'recipe card' with fields listed as 'ingredients' with measurement annotations. The layout invites scanning and mixing — understanding how entities combine.
influence.kanban: Kanban columns are styled as 'ingredient shelves' with individual recipe cards as isolated items on a white background, each with its own label and decorative border. This mirrors Sweetgreen's component-as-object-on-white presentation.
influence.directoryTree: The menu directory isolates each item on its own line with dot-leaders to metadata (price, availability) — the same ingredient-forward, individually labeled approach.

references.name: Milk Bar
references.takeaway: Mix a hand-lettered or script display font for headings with a clean geometric sans-serif for body text, creating the chalkboard-menu-meets-printed-receipt duality of a soda fountain counter.

influence.typography: Cherry Bomb One (bubbly hand-lettered display) is used for all headings, creating the chalkboard-menu feel. Lato (clean geometric sans-serif) handles body text, creating the printed-receipt clarity. Courier Prime (monospace) adds a third layer for data annotations, receipts, and status badges — the receipt-tape output. This three-layer typography system directly implements the chalkboard-menu-meets-printed-receipt duality.
influence.chalkboardHeaders: Every view opens with a dark chalkboard header bar that uses the hand-lettered Cherry Bomb One font, directly evoking Milk Bar's menu-board aesthetic with its hand-drawn display type.
influence.decorativeAccents: Price-tag status badges on project cards, striped awning patterns, and the cherry-red dot markers act as 'sticker and badge elements' scattered as decorative accents — mirroring Milk Bar's playful starburst and flavor callout elements.
